## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)
The global economic outlook for 2026 remains resilient yet slowing, influenced by a complex interplay of geopolitical events and technological advancements. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) projects global GDP growth at 3.0% for 2026, slightly lowered from previous estimates due to the offsetting impact of the war in the Middle East on an accelerated global technology cycle, particularly in artificial intelligence (AI). This indicates varied growth trajectories, with energy exporters and tech-integrated economies potentially benefiting, while oil-importing nations face headwinds.

Diplomatic efforts are underway with the 59th ASEAN Foreign Ministers' Meeting and related gatherings taking place in Manila from July 18-24, 2026, under the theme "Navigating Our Future, Together". The meetings aim to strengthen ASEAN cooperation and partnerships, with discussions planned on the implementation of ASEAN 2045: Our Shared Future and commemorating the 50th anniversary of the Treaty of Amity and Cooperation in Southeast Asia (TAC). Geopolitical volatility persists, with the ongoing US-Iran negotiations regarding the Strait of Hormuz facing hurdles due to sporadic hostilities, sustaining risks of disruption to shipping.

Further, the USMCA review deadline passed on July 1, 2026, with negotiations likely to extend beyond this date due to unresolved disputes. The 2026 NATO Summit in Ankara, Turkiye, held on July 7-8, acted as a stress test for alliance cohesion amid internal tensions over burden-sharing and defense spending. Global leaders continue to address the challenges of geoeconomic fragmentation, AI disruption, and energy volatility, recognizing them as core operating environment factors.

## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)
### Americas
In the Americas, Canada's "Combatting Hate Act" (Bill C-9) officially came into force on July 18, 2026, introducing new criminal offenses and escalating prison sentences for hate-motivated conduct. This legislation also criminalizes the public display of certain symbols used to promote hatred and protects access to places of worship and community spaces. The new law represents a significant expansion of Canada's criminal justice framework, arriving during a year of sweeping federal legislative changes.

In the United States, flags are being flown at half-staff until sunset on July 18, 2026, in honor of U.S. Senator Lindsey Graham, who passed away on July 11. Furthermore, the U.S. House Transportation and Infrastructure Committee unanimously passed the Water Resources Development Act of 2026 (WRDA 2026), a bipartisan bill focused on environmental protection, port investments, and flood management. The James S. McDonnell Planetarium in St. Louis, Missouri, reopened on July 18 after significant technological upgrades, now featuring the GOTO Chiron III Hybrid Star Projector, marking it as the largest and most advanced hybrid planetarium in the nation.

Meanwhile, Latin America is experiencing significant weather events, with heavy snowfall impacting ski resorts in South America, particularly in Chile, where upwards of 200 inches of snow are forecasted over the coming days, leading to road closures. Chile's President José Antonio Kast warned of widespread flooding risk in Santiago due to heavy rainfall. Additionally, the U.S. will meet Canada in the semifinals of the FIBA U17 Women's World Cup on Saturday, July 18, after defeating Slovenia.

### Europe
In Europe, Germany and France are set to deepen their nuclear cooperation, with Germany participating in a French military nuclear exercise later this year to strengthen future deterrence and develop a shared doctrine. This move highlights Europe's broader shift towards greater security independence. Meanwhile, the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control (ECDC) has warned of rising drug-resistant gonorrhea in Europe, posing a significant challenge for future treatment options.

In the United Kingdom, Andy Burnham is poised to become the new leader of the governing Labour Party, clearing his path to become prime minister next week. London is also preparing for a National March for Palestine on Saturday, July 18, with the Metropolitan Police imposing conditions on the demonstration to mitigate potential disruption. Prince Harry provided a rare update on King Charles' health, stating his father is "doing great" after a family reunion.

In sports, France and England will face each other in the 2026 FIFA World Cup third-place playoff in Miami on Saturday, July 18, marking Didier Deschamps' final match as France's coach. The Tour de France 2026 continues with Stage 14 on July 18, a demanding 155.3km mountain stage from Mulhouse to Le Markstein–Fellering in the Vosges Mountains.

### Asia-Pacific
In the Asia-Pacific region, China has announced an increase in domestic retail petrol and diesel prices starting July 18, attributing the adjustment to upward swings in global crude oil prices amid the US-Iran conflict. Additionally, Chinese President Xi Jinping addressed the World Artificial Intelligence Conference (WAIC) in Shanghai, advocating for global cooperation in AI development and offering China as a leader in a new global AI order. This comes as a landslide in Chongqing's Pengshui County, southwest China, killed at least eight people and left 34 missing on July 17, with President Xi urging comprehensive inspections.

Japan enacted a controversial law on July 17 that prohibits the desecration of its national flag, with penalties up to two years in prison or a fine of ¥200,000. This move by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's right-wing agenda has sparked concerns among opponents regarding freedom of speech. Furthermore, Japan's parliament revised the Imperial House Law to insist on male-only succession for the imperial family, a decision raising concerns for the shrinking family and prompting protests from those advocating for female succession. A fatal bus crash in May, carrying a high school soft tennis club, has led to efforts to improve the safety of school club road trips across Japan, ahead of the summer vacation season.

In Australia, plans for a national solar PV recycling program are on hold following the suspension of its flagship pilot project in May due to a complaint regarding the procurement process. Meanwhile, the buy now pay later firm Zip has announced its withdrawal from New Zealand to concentrate on its operations in Australia and the United States, effective July 18. The Philippines is hosting the ASEAN Foreign Ministers' Meeting and related events from July 18-24, with US Secretary of State Marco Rubio expected to attend to strengthen Indo-Pacific partnerships.

### Africa
In Africa, Nelson Mandela International Day is being commemorated on July 18, 2026, with various initiatives across South Africa. The government, through the Department of Water and Sanitation, is launching a National Water Access Acceleration Programme in KwaZulu-Natal, Eastern Cape, and Gauteng, aiming to expand access to safe water services in rural communities. This program includes the launch of 67 boreholes, symbolizing Nelson Mandela's 67 years of service. Additionally, the Minister of Electricity and Energy, Dr. Kgosientsho Ramokgopa, is unveiling a Solar Microgrid System at Mafikeng Provincial Hospital in the North West Province to enhance energy resilience and support critical medical operations.

In Nigeria, the Nigerian Bar Association (NBA) national election is proceeding as scheduled on Saturday, July 18, 2026. This decision by the National Executive Council (NEC) came after affirming the readiness of the Electoral Committee of the NBA (ECNBA) despite a temporary detention of the electronic voting service provider's Managing Director by the Department of State Services. The NBA President, Mazi Afam Osigwe, SAN, announced that the DSS and foreign observers would monitor the election to ensure transparency.

Meanwhile, a serious Ebola disease outbreak caused by the Bundibugyo virus remains active in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, with sustained transmission leading to a substantial increase in confirmed cases and deaths. As of July 15, 2026, a cumulative total of 2124 confirmed cases and 828 deaths have been reported in the DRC. Uganda, however, has reported no new cases since June 21, 2026, and has begun a 42-day period of enhanced surveillance before the outbreak can be declared over in the country.

## SECTION 3 — Gulf & MENA Region
The Gulf and MENA region remains in a state of heightened security and political tension. The United States has escalated its military actions against Iran, with U.S. forces destroying an Iranian surveillance tower on the Gulf of Oman coastline and conducting a seventh consecutive night of strikes aimed at degrading Iranian military capabilities. Iran, in response, has claimed attacks on U.S. assets in Bahrain, including a drone depot and an "artificial intelligence center," though these claims remain unverified. Iranian state media also reported strikes on civilian infrastructure within Iran, including bridges and an airport, with casualties reported.

The renewed conflict has largely halted commercial shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical energy waterway, sending oil prices surging. Iran has threatened "full-scale offensive operations" if U.S. strikes continue and has reportedly instructed Yemen's Houthi allies to prepare to close the Red Sea oil route if the U.S. attacks Iranian power infrastructure. This remains a developing story.

Economically, the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries are focusing on diversification and structural shifts away from oil dependence. Forecasts indicate GCC economic growth is expected to accelerate to approximately 4.5% in 2026, driven by expanding non-oil sectors like tourism, financial services, logistics, and technology. Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 and UAE's Vision 2031 are key initiatives in this transformation, with significant investments in infrastructure and industrial development.

## SECTION 5 — Tech Industry & Global Business Shifts
The global tech industry is witnessing significant shifts, with Apple overtaking Nvidia to become the world's most valuable company on July 18, 2026. Apple's market valuation reached approximately $4.88 trillion, surpassing Nvidia's $4.86 trillion, as investors broaden their focus beyond direct AI beneficiaries to companies positioned to monetize AI through services and ecosystem lock-in. This shift comes amidst a broader tech M&A boom driven by AI infrastructure, with deal value hitting $472 billion in the first five months of 2026, a 48% increase year-over-year.

In cybersecurity, the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has added a critical Microsoft SharePoint vulnerability (CVE-2026-58644) and an Oracle E-Business Suite flaw (CVE-2026-46817) to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, requiring federal agencies to apply fixes by July 18 and July 29, 2026, respectively. These additions highlight active exploitation in real-world attacks. Additionally, companies worldwide are experiencing a rise in AI-driven cyberattacks and ransomware incidents, with a South Asian IT services company targeted by a new ransomware family called Spirals in June 2026.

Hon Hai Precision Industry Co. (Foxconn) announced it expects to begin small-volume production of AI servers at its Texas plant within the next two months, utilizing humanoid robots and robotic arms developed with Nvidia Corp. for assembly. This move is part of a broader push to establish overseas high-tech parks and leverage AI in manufacturing. Tech industry M&A activity remains concentrated in larger transactions, particularly in sectors related to AI and digital infrastructure, even as broader deal volumes are subdued.

## SECTION 6 — Science & Research Breakthroughs
In science and research, ETH Zurich has achieved a significant milestone by unifying climate and Earth observation data, copying approximately 100 petabytes of publicly available data from NASA to the Swiss National Supercomputing Centre (CSCS). This initiative aims to provide vast amounts of high-quality data for training AI-powered weather models and enhancing climate research. Simultaneously, medical researchers in Taiwan have created a comprehensive gut microbiome database covering nearly all age groups of Taiwanese, marking a breakthrough in public health research that could lead to improved diagnostic and health management strategies.

In physics, the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) at CERN has entered a long shutdown for an ambitious upgrade, transforming into the High-Luminosity Large Hadron Collider (HL-LHC) by around 2030. This upgrade will significantly increase data delivery, allowing scientists to unlock further secrets of the Higgs boson and explore new frontiers in particle physics. Meanwhile, a study presented at the American Society of Retina Specialists (ASRS) annual meeting, concluding on July 18, 2026, highlighted that Vabysmo (faricimab) has shown to cut injections while preserving vision in patients with diabetic macular edema in a real-world setting.

Further medical advancements are being showcased at the American Head and Neck Society (AHNS) 12th International Conference on Head and Neck Cancer, taking place from July 18-22 in Boston. Roswell Park Comprehensive Cancer Center investigators are presenting 21 research works, including a novel AI and machine-learning platform for identifying oral lesions at elevated risk of malignant transformation.

## SECTION 7 — Global Health & Biosecurity Watch
A significant global health concern continues to be the ongoing Cyclospora outbreak in the United States, with a record number of cases reported across more than 30 states. Federal health officials have identified shredded iceberg lettuce from Mexico, served at Taco Bell locations in five U.S. states (Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and West Virginia), as a source of the parasite Cyclospora. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has warned consumers against consuming this lettuce as the investigation continues to determine if other sources or distribution channels are involved.

Meanwhile, the Ebola disease outbreak caused by the Bundibugyo virus remains active in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), with sustained transmission leading to a substantial increase in confirmed cases and deaths. As of July 15, 2026, the DRC has reported 2124 confirmed cases and 828 deaths. In contrast, Uganda has not reported any new cases since June 21, 2026, and has initiated a 42-day period of enhanced surveillance, moving towards declaring the end of the outbreak. This remains a developing story.

The World Health Organization (WHO) continues to address global health challenges, with a new report from July 8, 2026, warning that cancer cases could nearly double by 2050 without urgent action to strengthen prevention, early diagnosis, and treatment. The WHO also highlights stark inequalities in cancer survival between high and low-income countries. Additionally, the organization hosted a webinar on May 28, 2026, focusing on advancing heat-health preparedness during mass gatherings, providing practical tools and early warning systems to mitigate risks associated with extreme heat.

Global security threats persist with ongoing efforts to combat terrorism and address cybersecurity vulnerabilities. The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has issued alerts regarding actively exploited flaws in Microsoft SharePoint Server and Oracle E-Business Suite, requiring federal agencies to apply patches by July 19 and July 18, 2026, respectively. These vulnerabilities, including a critical deserialization of untrusted data flaw in SharePoint, are being actively weaponized by attackers in real-world intrusion campaigns.

In the realm of counter-terrorism, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) maintains a Worldwide Caution due to continued calls for violence from foreign terrorist organizations and the proliferation of false narratives. The FBI continues to make arrests for online threats, material support to terrorist groups, and international cybercrime networks. Recent press releases from the FBI highlight charges against individuals for plotting attacks and for their involvement in a large-scale cybercrime network targeting U.S. victims.

Concerns also remain regarding terrorist sanctuaries in Afghanistan, with reports indicating that groups like the TTP and Al-Qaeda operate with support networks within Taliban-controlled territory, enabling cross-border attacks, particularly against Pakistan. European officials and the international community are increasingly viewing this as a regional and global security problem, emphasizing the need for conditional engagement with the Taliban.

The global energy landscape is significantly impacted by the escalating conflict in the Middle East. Brent crude oil prices have surged, exceeding $86 per barrel, while WTI climbed to $80.99, as U.S.-Iran military exchanges intensify and target infrastructure across the Gulf. This surge is primarily driven by the "structural chokepoint" of the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately 20% of global oil and LNG flows, with markets pricing in the risk of closure. This remains a developing story.

Despite the volatility in oil markets, global investment in clean energy is projected to reach $2.2 trillion in 2026, nearly doubling fossil fuel spending, which is estimated at $1.2 trillion. The International Energy Agency's latest report indicates a significant shift towards sustainable energy sources, including renewables, nuclear power, and electrification. However, a report from Lazard notes that while unsubsidized renewable energy remains the most cost-competitive form of new-build generation, its levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) is rising due to higher capital costs, sustained interest rates, and supply chain repricing.

Extreme weather events continue to be a concern, with New York City Emergency Management issuing a weather alert for Saturday, July 18, anticipating severe thunderstorms, heavy rainfall, and localized flash flooding. The city is under a flood watch, with the strongest storms expected in the afternoon and early evening. Similarly, severe storms are possible in the Finger Lakes region, with lingering wildfire smoke also impacting air quality. In Washington, D.C., Mayor Bowser has activated a heat alert through Saturday, July 18, urging residents to prepare for high temperatures and humidity.

## SECTION 11 — Global Infrastructure & Mega Projects
Global infrastructure and mega-project developments are advancing across various sectors. In the United States, Houston Airports has secured over $126 million in federal funding for infrastructure projects at George Bush Intercontinental Airport (IAH) and William P. Hobby Airport (HOU). These funds will support upgrades to the Skyway train system, airfield drainage, and Terminal D's HVAC systems at IAH, and rehabilitate a primary runway at HOU, with construction expected to begin in 2027. New York Governor Kathy Hochul also announced the start of a $54.4 million steel repair and seismic retrofit project on the South Grand Island Bridges in Erie County, aiming to strengthen key structural elements and improve resilience against seismic events by late 2028.

In smart city developments, Nvidia is expanding its partnership with Toyota to power smart cities, traffic intelligence systems, and carmaking factories, integrating Nvidia's AI platforms into Toyota's experimental Woven City and leveraging its Isaac robotics platform for automotive software development. This collaboration highlights a shift towards ROI-driven, scalable infrastructure solutions like smart lighting, waste management, and environmental monitoring, with AI playing an increasingly crucial role in enhancing urban resilience.

Additionally, Chicago's Mayor Brandon Johnson joined CREATE Program partners to break ground on the Columbus Avenue Rail Grade Separation Project, a significant infrastructure investment to improve safety, reduce congestion, and strengthen emergency access in the Ashburn community. This project, which replaces an at-grade rail crossing with a new grade-separated structure, aims to eliminate conflicts between trains and vehicles. Meanwhile, Henry County, Georgia, is advancing two major roadway projects, the McDonough Parkway Extension and Flippen Road Extension, representing a $25 million investment in transportation infrastructure, with construction anticipated to be complete in the fall of 2026.

## SECTION 12 — Migration & Demographic Trends
Global displacement continues to be a pressing issue, with 41.6 million refugees reported worldwide at the beginning of 2026, nearly double the figure from a decade ago. Seven in ten refugees under UNHCR's mandate originate from just six countries: Afghanistan, South Sudan, Sudan, Syria, Ukraine, and Venezuela. Sudan, in particular, remains the world's largest internal displacement crisis, with over nine million people uprooted within the country. Recent escalations in Lebanon and Iran have also forced more than a million people from their homes.

In terms of border policies, the U.S. Trump administration is reviving the "public charge" rule, which could deny green cards to immigrants who use public benefits such as food stamps, Medicaid, and housing vouchers. This policy is set to be formally published on July 20 and take effect on September 18, reflecting a hard-line approach to curb both illegal and legal immigration. Simultaneously, several U.S. states, including Washington, Oregon, Maryland, New Jersey, Connecticut, Virginia, and New York, have passed laws to protect immigrant communities by limiting collaboration with federal immigration enforcement and strengthening data privacy.

Population statistics highlight a declining birthrate in Taiwan, which has experienced 30 consecutive months of population decline. The number of births in Taiwan is projected to drop below 100,000 for the first time this year, leading to calls for institutional reform to address issues like time poverty and housing affordability among young people. In the United States, population growth slowed sharply in 2025 due to an abrupt decline in immigration, with projections indicating further declines in 2026, which could result in historically low growth if natural change remains near current levels.

Prime Minister Narendra Modi flagged off India's first hydrogen-powered train, connecting Jind and Sonipat in Haryana, hailing it as a significant step towards green mobility and a successful "Make in India" initiative. President Murmu is set to embark on a three-nation tour, with Moldova and North Macedonia being her first destinations. NDA ministers convened a meeting to strategize ahead of the monsoon session of parliament.

## SECTION 16 — Complete MENA Strategic & Technology Developments
The Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region is undergoing a significant economic transformation and modernization drive, particularly in response to geopolitical risks and a global shift towards diversified economies. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) projects a contraction of 0.5% for the MENA region this year due to geopolitical unrest, but forecasts a strong rebound to 6.5% growth in 2027, contingent on the resolution of the geopolitical crisis around Iran. Notably, Iraq, Kuwait, and Qatar are expected to experience steep contractions in 2026 due to disrupted energy production and supply chains, while Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates (UAE) are projected to show moderate growth before accelerating above the regional average.

Technologically, the MENA region is heavily investing in sovereign technologies beyond Artificial Intelligence (AI), including biotechnology, quantum computing, nuclear energy, and zero-trust cybersecurity, all aligned with national industrial and security strategies. Saudi Arabia is reinforcing its role as a global cybersecurity hub by hosting the world's largest cybersecurity challenge, "Capture the Flag," at Black Hat Middle East and Africa 2026 in December. This initiative aims to develop a strong digital ecosystem and attract international expertise.

The UAE is marking its Union Pledge Day on July 18, commemorating the signing of the Declaration of the Union in 1971, which symbolizes its 55-year journey of development and global leadership in science, technology, and sustainability. The UAE government significantly increased capital spending in Q1 2026, investing in non-financial assets like infrastructure, digital transformation, renewable energy, and advanced industrial projects to support non-oil growth. This strategic deployment of capital reflects a deliberate acceleration of its economic diversification efforts. Iraq is also advancing efforts to expand oil production and reduce its reliance on the Strait of Hormuz by studying alternative export pipelines and attracting foreign investment.

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News
**What Happened:** The United States has significantly escalated its military actions against Iran, targeting Iranian infrastructure across the Gulf and destroying a surveillance tower on the Gulf of Oman coastline. Iran, in response, has claimed attacks on U.S. assets in Bahrain, including a drone depot and an "artificial intelligence center," though these claims are currently unverified. This escalation has largely halted commercial shipping traffic through the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz, leading to a surge in global oil prices.

**Root Causes:** The immediate root cause is the collapse of a ceasefire agreement between the U.S. and Iran, which occurred after Iran reportedly struck ships in the Strait of Hormuz on July 7, prompting a U.S. military response. This breakdown of the agreement has led to a cycle of retaliatory strikes. Underlying this is a prolonged period of geopolitical tension between the two nations, marked by a history of disputes over regional influence, nuclear ambitions, and maritime security in the Persian Gulf.

**Historical Background:** The U.S. and Iran have a long history of strained relations, with significant flashpoints including the 1979 Iranian Revolution, the Iran-Iraq War, and ongoing disagreements over Iran's nuclear program. Tensions in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global oil supplies, have frequently flared, with past incidents involving attacks on commercial vessels and threats of closure by Iran. The current conflict follows a period of renewed U.S. military presence and a reimposed blockade of Iranian ports, further exacerbating the situation.

**Key Actors:** The primary actors are the United States and Iran, specifically the U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) and Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC). Other implicated parties include Gulf countries hosting U.S. military bases (such as Bahrain, Kuwait, and Qatar) that have reportedly been targeted by Iranian forces, and Yemen's Houthi rebels, who Iran has reportedly instructed to be ready to close the Red Sea oil route.

**Strategic Importance:** This escalating conflict holds immense strategic importance due to its direct impact on global energy markets and international security. The Strait of Hormuz is vital for the transit of approximately 20% of global oil and LNG. Disruptions here have immediate and severe consequences for global fuel prices and economic stability. The expansion of targets to include infrastructure represents a dangerous escalation that could broaden the conflict's scope and draw in more regional and international actors, with potential for wider economic and geopolitical instability.

**Possible Long-Term Consequences:** Long-term consequences could include sustained high global energy prices, increased investment in diversifying energy sources and alternative shipping routes to mitigate reliance on the Strait of Hormuz, and a deepening of geopolitical divisions in the Middle East. There is a risk of a broader regional conflict, potentially involving U.S. allies, leading to widespread humanitarian crises and further destabilization. The conflict could also accelerate defense spending and technological competition in the region. This remains a developing story.

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)
*   **1870:** The First Vatican Council decreed the doctrine of Papal infallibility, formally establishing that the Pope cannot err when speaking on issues of morality and faith.
*   **1925:** Adolf Hitler published the first volume of his autobiographical manifesto, "Mein Kampf," while serving a prison sentence for treason.
*   **1942:** The German Me-262, the world's first jet-propelled aircraft to fly in combat, made its first flight.
*   **1968:** Intel, the world's largest semiconductor chip manufacturer, was founded in Mountain View, California, by Gordon Moore and Robert Noyce.
*   **1969:** The "Football War" between Honduras and El Salvador ended, four days after it began, having resulted in significant human and economic costs, including the displacement of an estimated 300,000 Salvadorans.
*   **1976:** Nadia Comăneci of Romania became the first person in Olympic Games history to score a perfect 10 in gymnastics at the 1976 Summer Olympics.
*   **1994:** A massive car bomb killed 96 people in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
*   **2013:** The city of Detroit, Michigan, declared bankruptcy, becoming the largest municipal entity in the United States to do so, with up to $20 billion in debt.

---

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)
*   **1857:** The Universities of Bombay (now Mumbai), Calcutta (now Kolkata), and Madras (now Chennai) were established, marking significant milestones in higher education in British India.
*   **1909:** Vishnu Dey, a renowned Bengali poet and Gyanpeeth awardee, was born.
*   **1947:** The Indian Independence Act 1947 was given royal assent and came into force, setting the stage for India's independence from British colonial rule on August 15, 1947, and the creation of Pakistan.
*   **1980:** Rohini 1, India's first indigenous satellite, was successfully launched by the SLV-3 from Sriharikota Range (SHAR), making India the sixth nation to put a satellite into orbit.
*   **1993:** Girilal Jain, a prominent editor of The Times of India, passed away at the age of 71.

---

## SECTION 21 — This Week in Review (Last 7 Days)
Over the past week (July 11-18, 2026), global headlines have been dominated by the escalating conflict between the United States and Iran in the Middle East. Both nations have engaged in retaliatory strikes, with Iran targeting American military facilities and the U.S. launching missiles and drones at Iranian targets, signifying the definitive end of last month's truce. The U.S. has renewed a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, prompting Iranian officials to assert their control over the waterway and raising concerns about global energy supplies and prices.

In other significant developments, a widespread cyclosporiasis outbreak has affected at least 32 U.S. states, sickening thousands, with health officials investigating potential sources like contaminated salad greens. Politically, a bipartisan housing affordability bill in the U.S. became law without presidential signature, reflecting efforts to address rising home prices. Ukraine has urged allies to expedite air defense support following a large-scale Russian missile and drone attack across the country.

Meanwhile, in Australia, mysterious metal spheres washed ashore on Forrest Beach, later identified by the space agency as pressure vessels from a space launch vehicle. In India, monsoon floods have exposed deep frustrations over government planning and preparedness in vulnerable communities. The European Union has preliminarily found Meta in breach of the Digital Services Act, opening the door to a multi-billion euro fine over allegations of designing platforms to exploit young users.

---

## SECTION 22 — This Month in Review (From 1st of Current Month)
The first half of July 2026 has been marked by a significant escalation in the U.S.-Iran conflict, with both nations engaging in retaliatory strikes that definitively ended last month's truce. Iran targeted commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, prompting U.S. strikes on Iranian military facilities, leading to a renewed naval blockade of the Strait. This remains a developing story, with implications for global energy markets.

Other key developments include a widespread cyclosporiasis outbreak in at least 32 U.S. states, with thousands of confirmed cases and health officials investigating contaminated salad greens as a potential source. In Canada, Prime Minister Mark Carney delivered a Canada Day speech promoting unity, while the U.S. officially announced it would not renew the Canada-United States-Mexico (CUSMA) trade agreement, sparking uncertainty around free trade in North America.

Globally, record-high sea surface temperatures have been observed, with experts warning that many major global cities are unprepared for the unprecedented scale of extreme weather events. In India, monsoon floods have caused widespread disruption and exposed frustrations over government planning. The European Commission has initiated action against Meta for alleged breaches of the Digital Services Act, specifically concerning platform design that may exploit young users.

## SECTION 24 — Key Global Indicators Snapshot
**Crude oil benchmark price (Brent/WTI):** Brent crude settled at $88.10 per barrel on July 17, up 4.59% from the previous day, reaching its highest level in over a month. WTI crude for August delivery gained 4.48% to settle at $82.49 a barrel on July 17. The surge is attributed to heightened U.S.-Iran hostilities and ongoing supply risks in the Middle East.
**US Dollar index direction:** The US Dollar Index (DXY) traded near 100.765 as of July 17, showing a slight decrease of 0.01% from the previous session. The dollar has weakened by 0.09% over the past month but is up 2.31% over the last 12 months. Technical indicators suggest the DXY is testing the integrity of its May uptrend.
**Major currency signals:** The Brazilian real has appreciated by nearly 11% against the U.S. dollar since the beginning of the year, marking the best performance among significant currencies in 2026. The Australian dollar and the Norwegian krone have also seen gains of almost 7%. Conversely, the Pound is expected to experience volatility and modest gains against the US Dollar, while the Japanese Yen may appreciate gradually.
**Global equity trend:** Global equity markets are near record highs in early July, having recovered from a selloff in March at the outset of the war in Iran. However, there is a deepening selloff in semiconductor stocks, which is weighing on Asian equities. The Nasdaq closed in the red for a second straight day, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index slid to its lowest levels since May.
**Inflation indicators:** The International Monetary Fund (IMF) in its July 2026 World Economic Outlook flagged concerns over rising inflation pressures, estimating global inflation to hit 4.7% this year. S&P Global Market Intelligence has reduced its consumer price inflation forecasts for 2026-27, although they remain above February's pre-conflict projections. Lower commodity price assumptions have contributed to improved inflation outlooks for net oil-importing economies.

---

## SECTION 25 — Strategic Insight / Underreported Development
An underreported strategic development involves the escalating pressure on media in the United States, particularly from the Trump administration. Four New York Times journalists have been subpoenaed by the Justice Department, some receiving the subpoenas at their homes, after reporting on security concerns surrounding President Trump's new Qatari-gifted Air Force One. The subpoenas seek to compel their testimony before a federal grand jury regarding an alleged violation of criminal law.

This action is seen by the New York Times as an extraordinary escalation in efforts to threaten and intimidate independent news organizations, aiming to prevent the public from accessing information about government operations. The Justice Department has previously issued and withdrawn similar subpoenas to other major news outlets this year, indicating a pattern of using federal power against the press. The timing also coincides with concerns about foreign election threats and the potential misuse of artificial intelligence in spreading misinformation ahead of the 2026 elections, highlighting a broader environment where media freedom and national security intersect.

---
